1.synthesis:
David hears that the Philistines are raiding Keilah and threshing its grain, and he inquires of YHWH whether to go and strike them (v. 2). YHWH says go. David’s men are afraid — they are already afraid in Judah; going to Keilah means fighting the Philistines in the open (v. 3). David inquires again; again YHWH confirms. They go, strike the Philistines, and save Keilah (vv. 4–5). When Saul hears that David is at Keilah — a walled city he can besiege — he calls for a march: YHWH has given him into my hand (v. 7). David, with the ephod available through Abiathar, inquires again: will Saul come? Will the citizens of Keilah hand me over? YHWH answers both questions: yes to Saul; yes to Keilah (vv. 11–12). David and his men — now six hundred — leave Keilah. Saul abandons the march.

The double inquiry-and-answer sequence is the pattern that will recur throughout David’s fugitive period: before every major decision, YHWH is consulted through Abiathar’s ephod. The contrast with Saul — who cannot successfully inquire of YHWH (28:6) — is already implicit. The citizens of Keilah’s willingness to hand David over to Saul in exchange for being left alone is not surprising — they have been saved by the fugitive, but the cost of harboring him is too high. David does not condemn them; he simply leaves. The man who saved the city cannot safely stay in it. This is the fugitive king’s condition: serving those who cannot protect him in return.
1.historical_context: Keilah was a Judean town in the Shephelah, south of Adullam, that was strategically vulnerable to Philistine raiders who would take the grain at threshing time — the most profitable moment for raiders, when the harvest was concentrated at the threshing floors. David’s military action to save Keilah anticipates his eventual royal role as Israel’s protector; his wilderness band is already functioning as a defensive force for Judah. The ephod with Urim and Thummim, brought by Abiathar (v. 6), provides David with the oracular access that distinguishes his wilderness period from Saul’s abandoned relationship with YHWH.
1.literary_structure: Chapter 23 develops the pattern of double or triple inquiry before action that characterizes the David narratives. The Keilah episode (vv. 1–13) uses the inquiry pattern three times: before the attack (vv. 2, 4) and before the departure (vv. 10–12). The three-part inquiry demonstrates both David’s consistent dependence on YHWH’s direction and YHWH’s willingness to provide specific, actionable answers. The contrast between Saul’s court​ (“YHWH has given him into my hand” — Saul’s self-assured but wrong reading of the situation) and David’s camp (patient inquiry, actionable answers) is the structural counterpoint of the chapter.
1.theological_application: David’s double inquiry before the Keilah attack — asking again when the men’s fear provides a plausible reason not to go — models the difference between asking once for guidance and persisting in inquiry when the answer is inconvenient or frightening. YHWH does not rebuke the second inquiry; he answers it with the same direction as the first. The willingness to re-ask rather than assuming one answer settles all subsequent questions reflects a genuine desire to know YHWH’s will rather than to confirm a predetermined preference. Communities of faith that inquire of YHWH earnestly, including when the answer is costly or frightening, will find the pattern of Keilah: the same direction, confirmed, with enough specificity (yes Saul will come; yes Keilah will hand you over) to enable responsive action.
1.christology: David saving Keilah from the Philistines only to find that Keilah would hand him to his enemies images the pattern of Christ’s ministry: healing and saving those who cannot fully receive him, who will not or cannot protect him from the powers pursuing him. John 1:11: “He came to his own, and his own people did not receive him.” The cities that were healed and fed — Chorazin, Bethsaida, Capernaum — did not turn (Matt 11:20–24). David leaves without condemnation; Jesus pronounces woe. Both know what the cities will do; both go to save them anyway. The fugitive king who serves those who cannot shelter him in return is the pattern of the one who came not to be served but to serve.

14.pericope_label: David in the Wilderness of Ziph
14.synthesis:
David lives in the wilderness strongholds of the Ziph wilderness while Saul seeks him daily; “but God did not give him into his hand” (v. 14). Jonathan finds David at Horesh and strengthens his hand in God (v. 16). Jonathan’s words are a theological declaration and a covenant renewal: “Do not fear, for the hand of Saul my father shall not find you. You shall be king over Israel, and I shall be next to you. Saul my father also knows this” (v. 17). They renew their covenant before YHWH and part — their last meeting before Jonathan’s death. The Ziphites, meanwhile, betray David to Saul (vv. 19–20), who closes in from one side of a mountain while David is on the other side. The pursuit is interrupted by a Philistine raid that requires Saul to break off (vv. 27–28). David escapes to En-gedi.

The Horesh meeting is the last substantive encounter between David and Jonathan. The theological clarity of Jonathan’s declaration — “you shall be king; I shall be second; even my father knows this” — is the most explicit human acknowledgment in the narrative that YHWH’s purpose is certain. Jonathan, who will not live to see the fulfillment, names it as settled fact and then parts from David. The strengthening “in God” is specifically spiritual: Jonathan helps David’s soul find its footing in YHWH’s faithfulness rather than in circumstances. The subsequent events — Ziphite betrayal, narrowing pursuit, last-minute Philistine interruption — test that strengthening immediately.
14.historical_context: The Wilderness of Ziph is in the Judean desert south of Hebron; the Ziphites were inhabitants of this area who were ostensibly part of Judah but chose to betray their kinsman to Saul. The narrowness of the escape at the mountain (“Saul went on one side of the mountain, and David and his men on the other side of the mountain, hurrying to get away from Saul” — v. 26) is one of the most vivid proximity-to-disaster moments in the narrative. The place was subsequently called Sela-hammahlekoth (“the rock of parting” or “the rock of escape”) in memory of the close escape (v. 28).
14.literary_structure: Chapter 23’s second unit (vv. 14–29) juxtaposes the covenantal high point (Jonathan’s strengthening at Horesh) with the immediate threat of Ziphite betrayal and near-capture. The structure of encouragement followed by crisis followed by providential interruption (Philistine raid) is the pattern of providential preservation: the spiritual strengthening at Horesh is tested within the same chapter. YHWH does not give Saul the victory despite his best effort and informants’ help; the interruption is providential, not accidental.
14.theological_application: Jonathan’s coming to David “in the wilderness of Ziph at Horesh, and strengthened his hand in God” describes one of the most specific forms of spiritual care: going to the wilderness where the discouraged person is hiding and strengthening them in the one thing that will hold — not in circumstances, not in strategies, but “in God.” The distinction between strengthening someone in their circumstances (which may be genuinely terrible) and strengthening them in God (whose faithfulness does not depend on circumstances) is the difference between comfort that depends on the situation and comfort that persists through it. Jonathan’s ministry to David models what it means to be a friend who helps another person find their footing in YHWH when everything external is hostile.
14.christology: Jonathan’s final visit to David in the wilderness — strengthening, covenant renewal, departure — anticipates the post-resurrection appearances of Christ to the disciples in their discouraged hiding. The pattern of the one whose future is secure coming to the one whose future is uncertain, declaring what will be, and then departing is the shape of both moments. Jonathan says “you shall be king” and leaves; the risen Christ says “all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me” (Matt 28:18) and ascends. Both declarations of certain future are delivered in conditions of present danger and uncertainty; both are intended to “strengthen the hand” of those who remain in the wilderness after the speaker departs.
